stories.headline: RTD weighs eliminating 16th Street FreeRide shuttle, light-rail lines in up to $62 million in cuts
stories.summary:
The Regional Transportation District is considering eliminating Denver's free 16th Street shuttle, several light-rail lines and dozens of bus routes under proposed service cuts designed to save the financially ailing agency up to $62 million. Agency staffers compiled five scenarios with progressively larger reductions; all but the most modest two would fully discontinue the FreeRide, which logged 2.3 million riders last year and ranks among RTD's most-used routes.

The proposals also target the B, R, L and T light-rail lines and the Free MetroRide between Union and Civic stations. RTD board members stressed Tuesday night that the list is not a final vote item, with Director Patrick O'Keefe acknowledging the documents caused 'confusion and alarm' and saying the board has not settled on a course of action.

Mayor Mike Johnston's office pushed back sharply, calling the FreeRide 'the kind of thing we need more of if we want people to use public transportation, not less.' The Downtown Denver Partnership also urged RTD to weigh the shuttle's role in connecting workers, residents and visitors along the recently completed $175.4 million 16th Street renovation.

RTD faces a budget deficit exceeding $200 million and is conducting an 18-month Comprehensive Operational Analysis expected to produce final service recommendations later this year. Cutting the FreeRide alone would save an estimated $9.5 million annually, more than any other single route on the chopping block.

stories.headline: Sierra Space wins $798 million U.S. Space Force contract to build missile-tracking satellites
stories.summary:
Louisville-based Sierra Space has been awarded a $798 million contract to build missile-tracking satellites for the U.S. Space Force, with work to be performed at the company's Denver-metro area facilities. The award represents one of the largest defense contracts secured by a Colorado aerospace firm in recent years.

Sierra Space, which is developing commercial space infrastructure including the Dream Chaser spaceplane, has been expanding its footprint along Colorado's Front Range. The satellite contract adds a significant national security dimension to the company's portfolio alongside its civil and commercial space programs.

The Space Force contract is expected to support hundreds of high-skill engineering and manufacturing jobs in the Denver metro area. Colorado's aerospace sector is one of the state's largest economic drivers, anchored by a cluster of government, military and commercial space companies concentrated between Denver and Colorado Springs.

stories.headline: Denver man charged with first-degree murder after wife found dead in backyard
stories.summary:
A Denver man faces a first-degree murder charge after his wife was found dead Sunday morning in the backyard of their home in the 1500 block of South Xavier Street. Officers were called shortly before noon after Herman Antonio Sisneros, 41, contacted emergency services; Denver's STAR mental health response team was also dispatched and discovered the woman with an abdominal wound.

Investigators said the cause of the wound was initially undetermined. Sisneros was taken into custody at the scene after his wife's death was deemed suspicious. Police noted that officers had responded to the same address the previous day for a domestic disturbance call.

Sisneros was booked into the Denver Downtown Detention Center. In addition to first-degree murder, he also faces charges of attempted first-degree murder and escape from jail, though authorities have not elaborated on what those additional charges stem from. His wife has not been publicly identified.

stories.headline: Federal judge takes under advisement injunction request in lawsuit alleging Denver targeted employees for political retaliation
stories.summary:
A federal judge has taken under advisement a request for a preliminary injunction filed by Jessica Calderon, a former Denver city official who alleges she was deliberately targeted for termination as part of the city's 2025 layoffs due to political retaliation and sex discrimination. Calderon, who served as director of operations and innovation in the Mayor's Office of Social Equity & Innovation, won a temporary restraining order blocking her termination earlier this year.

Nearly six hours of testimony were heard Tuesday in U.S. District Court from Calderon and two of her former supervisors, Chief Equity Officer Ben Sanders and Deputy Director Brian Firooz, both named as defendants. Recorded conversations between Calderon and Firooz were submitted as evidence alongside performance evaluations that cited both her contributions and instances of communication lapses.

Calderon's lawsuit, filed in June 2025, alleges she was denied promotions given to male colleagues and that Sanders confronted her over her ties to Latinos United Neighbors Association and its founder, who ran against Mayor Mike Johnston in 2023. Sanders testified he offered her advice on 'politics and optics' rather than openly confronting her about the association.

U.S. District Court Judge Nina Y. Wang said the court would issue a written order 'as soon as possible.' The city argued that granting the injunction could trigger copycat litigation and erode fiscal integrity, but Judge Wang called those concerns speculative and said future litigants would still need to satisfy the legal standards for relief on their own facts.

stories.headline: Tuberculosis case confirmed at Aurora ICE detention center, triggering public health investigation
stories.summary:
At least one confirmed case of active tuberculosis has been identified at the ICE detention facility in Aurora, prompting the Arapahoe County Health Department to issue a public health order requiring the facility to provide information needed to investigate the outbreak. Colorado law mandates that local public health agencies investigate every confirmed case of active tuberculosis, identify potentially exposed individuals and take steps to prevent further transmission.

The Denver Health Tuberculosis Clinic is assisting in the investigation. Officials have not disclosed how many detainees or staff may have been exposed or what stage the investigation has reached. The Aurora ICE facility has been a focal point of immigration enforcement debates in Colorado, and advocacy groups have raised ongoing concerns about health conditions inside the center.

Denver immigration activist Dana Miller, who has been tracking ICE expansion across the state, said the situation illustrates broader problems with civil immigration detention, calling it 'detention without due process.' Health authorities have not indicated whether additional cases are suspected.

stories.headline: Denver DMV to end rolling office closures beginning in August
stories.summary:
Denver will discontinue its rotating DMV branch closures starting Aug. 3, ending a cost-saving measure that had frustrated residents for roughly two years. The policy, which closed one of four Denver Motor Vehicle branches on a four-week rotating basis, was adopted in 2024 to offset city expenses tied to housing thousands of migrants who arrived beginning in late 2022.

City Treasurer and interim DMV Director Sophia Hassman said the change will create 'more stability and consistency' for both customers and staff. The city has been hiring additional DMV employees in advance of the full reopening. All locations will be closed one day in late July for staff training before the regular schedule resumes.

As part of the restructuring, the Tremont location in the Elbra M. Wedgeworth Municipal Building will be converted into a commercial vehicle dealer and fleet center, serving only dealers and commercial fleet customers going forward. Online services and DMV kiosks, which handle most registration and title transactions, will remain continuously available.

stories.headline: Denver eliminates local childcare business license to reduce barriers and costs
stories.summary:
Denver City Council has unanimously voted to eliminate the city's local childcare business license, a step officials say will reduce redundant regulatory hurdles without diminishing child safety. State licensing requirements — including health and safety inspections, staff training standards and child-to-adult ratio rules — remain in place.

City officials said the change is intended to chip away at barriers that have suppressed the supply of childcare providers. The average cost of infant care in Denver runs about $20,000 per year, and officials hope reducing administrative burdens will encourage new businesses to open. The eliminated local license carried fees ranging from $25 to $200.

Rhett Gutierrez, head of policy in the city's Office of Children Affairs, described the move as 'our first attempt' at addressing the childcare supply crisis rather than a comprehensive fix. Several council members cited personal experiences with the city's constrained childcare options during the committee process before the measure passed on consent.

stories.headline: Heat wave continues in Denver metro with 90s through the week and possible near-100 readings this weekend
stories.summary:
Denver and the broader metro area remain in the grip of an extended heat wave, with afternoon high temperatures expected to stay in the low-to-mid 90s through the end of the work week. Meteorologists are warning of possible highs approaching 100 degrees over the weekend before monsoon moisture is forecast to arrive next week and provide some relief.

The National Weather Service in Boulder issued an Ozone Action Day Alert for much of the Denver metro area Tuesday, covering Douglas, Jefferson, Denver, western Arapahoe, western Adams, Broomfield, Boulder, Larimer and Weld counties through 4 p.m. The worst air quality is expected along the foothills corridor from Denver north to Fort Collins. Tuesday's high of about 95 degrees is roughly five degrees above average for the date.

Health officials are urging residents — particularly those who are elderly, very young or without air conditioning — to stay hydrated, limit outdoor activity during peak afternoon hours and seek cooling centers. Afternoon wind gusts up to 34 mph are possible Tuesday, which could worsen fire weather conditions across parts of the state already dealing with multiple active wildfires.

stories.headline: Nuggets sign Euroleague defensive player of the year Alpha Diallo, a former Denver West prep star
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets have signed Alpha Diallo, who was named the Euroleague's defensive player of the year, adding a locally connected defensive standout to their roster. Diallo is a former Denver West High School player, making his signing a homecoming of sorts for the French-American guard.

Diallo spent several seasons developing in Europe after his college career, earning recognition as one of the continent's premier perimeter defenders. His Euroleague accolade signals readiness to contribute at the NBA level, and the Nuggets identified him as a defensive addition that fits their championship contention profile.

The signing adds depth to a Nuggets squad that has continued to build around two-time MVP Nikola Jokic. Denver's front office has shown an increasing willingness to look at European leagues for proven talent, and Diallo's defensive credentials address a position of need for the team.

stories.headline: Nuggets waive center Jonas Valanciunas before guarantee deadline, saving at least $8 million
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets have waived center Jonas Valanciunas before his contract became guaranteed, saving the franchise at least $8 million in salary obligations. The move is a significant roster and financial decision as the team manages its cap situation heading into the 2026-27 season.

Valanciunas, a veteran Lithuanian center, had joined the Nuggets as a depth option behind Nikola Jokic. His release suggests the front office preferred financial flexibility or has identified other roster priorities over retaining the veteran big man.

The waiving of Valanciunas, combined with the signing of Alpha Diallo, reflects an active offseason for the Nuggets as they reshape the roster around their core. The cap savings could be applied toward other acquisitions or retained as financial flexibility ahead of the season.

stories.headline: Sundae ice cream opens flagship downtown Denver store with in-house production and 'waffle theater'
stories.summary:
Mountain-town ice cream chain Sundae has opened its largest location in a 5,100-square-foot space on 16th Street in downtown Denver, featuring an in-house commercial kitchen visible to customers on three sides and a waffle cone-making 'theater' where patrons can watch cones being produced fresh. Owner Kent Beidel took the unusual step of manufacturing the product on-site in a high-rent downtown location rather than in a lower-cost facility.

The Denver Downtown Development Authority provided a $750,000 loan to support the project as part of a broader downtown business incentive program. Beidel, who founded the Loaded Joe's restaurant chain in Vail before pivoting to ice cream, said the downtown location could eventually supply multiple Denver-area stores from its commercial kitchen.

Sundae currently operates locations in Vail, Edwards, Glenwood Springs, Basalt and Snowmass. The flagship Denver store opened in early June and Beidel described growing momentum on 16th Street since the pedestrian mall's $175 million renovation was completed last year, saying foot traffic has 'perked up' noticeably in recent weeks.

stories.headline: CoPIRG calls for 'right to update' laws as electronic waste from expired software tops 1.7 billion pounds nationally
stories.summary:
Colorado consumer advocacy group CoPIRG staged a mock funeral for obsolete electronics Wednesday at Governors Park in Denver, using gravestones for discontinued Kindles, Spotify Car Things and old Google Nest thermostats to highlight what the group calls widespread waste created when manufacturers end software support for still-functional devices. A new CoPIRG Foundation report estimates 1.7 billion pounds of electronic waste has been generated nationwide by expired software and canceled cloud services since 2014, with Windows 10's expiration accounting for up to 1.6 billion pounds of that total.

CoPIRG Director Danny Katz said the most sustainable option is extending device life, not recycling, and called on manufacturers to publicly disclose at the point of sale how many years of software support a product will receive. The group is pressing companies to voluntarily negotiate with consumer advocates to extend support for older models.

If voluntary agreements are not reached, CoPIRG said it will pursue 'right to update' legislation from Colorado lawmakers, building on existing state laws that already provide 'right to repair' protections for wheelchairs and farm equipment. Colorado has been an early mover on right-to-repair policy, making it a likely legislative battleground for expanded consumer electronics protections.

weather.notable: Nearly 5 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: You remember being 12 when you're like, 'No one look at me or I'll kill myself.'

topStory.headline: Blanche Faces Senate Confirmation Hearing as Acting Attorney General Amid IRS Settlement Fallout
topStory.summary:
Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche appeared Wednesday before the Senate Judiciary Committee for his confirmation hearing to serve permanently as the nation's top law enforcement officer. Blanche, whom President Trump nominated last month following the April ouster of Pam Bondi, has served in an acting capacity since Bondi's firing and previously represented Trump as his personal defense attorney in multiple criminal cases.

Democrats were expected to press Blanche on what they characterize as politicized prosecutions, including the firing of career prosecutors, the indictment of the Southern Poverty Law Center, and criminal charges filed against former FBI Director James Comey. Senators on both sides of the aisle also planned to question him about the Justice Department's handling of the Jeffrey Epstein files and the controversial $1.8 billion 'anti-weaponization' fund created as part of a settlement resolving Trump's $10 billion civil lawsuit against the IRS over the leak of his tax returns.

The settlement, which Blanche approved, established a fund to compensate alleged victims of government weaponization and granted Trump, his two eldest sons, and related business entities immunity from future tax claims. Bipartisan criticism prompted Blanche to tell a House committee last month that the department was scrapping the payout plan, but he declined to put that commitment in writing, and the Justice Department refused a federal judge's request for Blanche and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent to sign a sworn declaration attesting to the fund's status.

On Monday, a federal judge in Miami issued a sharp rebuke of Trump, his lawyers, and the Justice Department over their conduct in the IRS case, finding the original lawsuit was filed for an improper purpose. Judge Kathleen Williams questioned whether Blanche violated ethical rules governing conflicts of interest and directed a copy of her order to the State Bar of New York. The ruling also barred Blanche and the Justice Department's No. 3 official, Stanley Woodward, from citing provisions of the deal as evidence of a settlement in any formal proceeding.

Despite the turbulence, most observers expect Blanche to be narrowly confirmed by the Republican-led Senate. The GOP's margin on the Judiciary Committee narrowed following the sudden death Saturday of Sen. Lindsey Graham of South Carolina, a senior committee member and staunch Blanche supporter. Sen. Thom Tillis of North Carolina, one of the more skeptical Republicans, told reporters Tuesday he was nonetheless leaning toward supporting the nomination, barring new revelations at the hearing.
